pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient has ESRD (N18.6) with dependence on hemodialysis (Z99.2) and CKD stage 5 (N18.5) complicated by CKD-related anemia (D63.1) and persistent fatigue (R53.83). Following transplant committee review and completion of required pre-transplant testing, renal transplantation with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) is requested; perioperative planning accounts for comorbid atherosclerotic heart disease (I25.10) and hypertension (I10).

pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved renal function with ability to discontinue chronic dialysis and improved functional status while maintaining careful perioperative cardiovascular management.

225.bio_narrative: Meredith Grey is a 45-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who has remained actively engaged in her health care while balancing a demanding professional life and family responsibilities. Over the past several years she has managed chronic cardiovascular conditions including atherosclerotic heart disease (I25.10), essential hypertension (I10), and hyperlipidemia (E78.5). She has been consistent with prescribed risk-reduction therapy including metoprolol, losartan, and atorvastatin, and previously completed a course of cardiac rehabilitation to support endurance and conditioning. In late 2025 and early 2026 she began to notice worsening fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and generalized weakness that were no longer explained by work stress alone. At the 01-20-2026 specialist visit she reported persistent low energy, difficulty completing routine daily activities, and prolonged recovery after exertion. Subsequent evaluation demonstrated progression to chronic kidney disease stage 5 (N18.5) and end stage renal disease (N18.6) requiring ongoing in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2). HPI: Patient presents for routine follow-up and medication review for chronic conditions including atherosclerotic heart disease, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. She reports good adherence to Lopressor, Cozaar, and Lipitor with no side effects. Denies chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, syncope, or palpitations.

Review of Systems: Negative for fever, cough, chest pain, edema, dizziness, or new exercise intolerance. Reports regular physical activity and stable appetite.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing female in no acute distress. Cardiovascular exam: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs, no peripheral edema. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Neurologic exam grossly intact.

Plan: Continue metoprolol, losartan, atorvastatin. Reinforce heart-healthy diet and activity. Routine labs to be monitored through primary care. Return in 6 months or sooner for symptoms.

images.findings: Non-contrast CT obtained for preoperative planning prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy. Native kidneys are small consistent with chronic medical renal disease; no hydronephrosis or acute abdominal process. Iliac vessels demonstrate mild calcified atherosclerosis but preserved caliber suitable for transplant anastomosis planning.

LVEF 55–60% with mild concentric LVH and grade I diastolic dysfunction; mild MR; no pericardial effusion. Study obtained as cardiac clearance supporting preoperative planning for renal transplant in setting of I25.10 and I10.

reports.results: CBC: Hgb 9.2 g/dL, MCV 89 fL; iron indices adequate; B12/folate normal. Impression consistent with anemia in CKD (D63.1) contributing to fatigue (R53.83) in patient with CKD stage 5/ESRD (N18.5/N18.6) on dialysis (Z99.2).
reports.notes: Obtained as part of pre-transplant workup to characterize anemia prior to requested transplant surgery.

reports.results: HIV Ag/Ab non-reactive; HBsAg non-reactive; HBsAb reactive; HCV Ab non-reactive; CMV IgG positive; EBV IgG positive.
reports.notes: Completed in preparation for requested renal transplant (CPT 50360) to inform prophylaxis and immunosuppression planning.

Plan: Continue aspirin and atorvastatin. Start/continue metoprolol for symptom control and prescribe PRN nitroglycerin with clear ER precautions. Continue losartan for BP control. He notes reduced exercise tolerance and mild shortness of breath with stairs. Denies chest pain, syncope, or fever. Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and leg swelling. Intermittent nausea but no vomiting today. No dysuria or flank pain. No cough or wheezing. No focal neurologic deficits.

223.bio_narrative: Caroline Channing is a 40-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas who has been managing progressive kidney disease that culminated in end stage renal disease (N18.6) with dependence on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2). She works in a customer-facing retail role with variable shifts, and she relies on a close circle of friends for transportation support on dialysis days when she feels fatigued. Caroline reports strong day-to-day adherence to her renal regimen, including phosphate binder with meals and a renal multivitamin, and she keeps a careful, renal-friendly low-sodium diet. She drinks alcohol only occasionally at social gatherings and has never used tobacco. Family history she finds most relevant is hypertension in multiple relatives and a paternal uncle with kidney failure requiring dialysis. Her dialysis course has been complicated by vascular access problems. An AV fistula was created on 05-15-2025 with the goal of durable access. Beginning in early 2026, she developed recurrent problems at the dialysis unit: difficult cannulations, repeated low arterial/venous pressure alarms, and an inability to maintain the blood flow needed to complete treatments. images.type: Duplex ultrasound - Hemodialysis access (AV fistula) evaluation
images.findings: Duplex Doppler evaluation demonstrates diminished access volume flow with dampened outflow waveforms. There are elevated velocities across a short-segment outflow narrowing with post-stenotic turbulence consistent with hemodynamically significant stenosis. Draining vein remains compressible without acute thrombosis and there is no perivascular fluid collection. Findings correlate with repeated dialysis low-flow alarms and support need for alternative reliable access including requested tunneled catheter (CPT 36558).

224.bio_narrative: Ross Geller is a 57-year-old Caucasian male living in downtown San Antonio, Texas, who has been managing end stage renal disease (N18.6) with dependence on maintenance hemodialysis (Z99.2) for several years. He receives dialysis through a surgically created arteriovenous fistula (created 06-20-2023) and has remained consistent with scheduled sessions. Over time, he has continued to experience fatigue and “crash” symptoms on dialysis days, with intermittent muscle cramping and occasional post-dialysis lightheadedness. His chronic kidney disease has been accompanied by anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1), treated with epoetin alfa administered through the dialysis unit, and renal mineral-bone disorder characterized by secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81) and abnormal phosphorus metabolism (E83.39), managed with cinacalcet and sevelamer carbonate alongside strict dietary counseling. In early 2026, his care team at Houston Methodist Hospital intensified pre-transplant planning. 226.bio_narrative: Ross Geller is a 39-year-old male living in Houston, Texas who works in museum and field-based roles that sometimes require travel and irregular meals. Over the past several months he has experienced intermittent right-sided flank discomfort with urinary hesitancy and a reduced urinary stream. Symptoms are often accompanied by intermittent nausea and occasional vomiting (R11.2), especially during pain flares, which has affected his ability to concentrate at work and maintain his usual routine. He denies tobacco and illicit drug use and drinks alcohol minimally in social settings on weekends. He routinely walks for exercise and uses relaxation techniques when anxious during medical visits. He first sought specialty evaluation on 01-20-2026 for persistent flank pain and urinary hesitancy (R10.9, R39.11) with concern for obstruction and hydronephrosis. The working diagnoses include hydronephrosis with renal/ureteral calculous obstruction (N13.2) and unspecified hydronephrosis (N13.30). A blood test was obtained on 01-15-2026 as part of the initial workup. 229.bio_narrative: Kendall Roy is a 40-year-old Caucasian male living in Austin, Texas, who has been followed by Dr. Logan Kent, MD at Austin General Healthcare for persistent low back pain that has gradually progressed to include right-sided radicular symptoms. He reports a pattern of low back pain aggravated by prolonged sitting and transitions from sitting to standing, with intermittent shooting pain down the posterior right leg and episodes of numbness/tingling. He has remained engaged in care and notes that symptoms have continued despite completing a supervised physical therapy program and maintaining a consistent home exercise routine. Over late 2025 and early 2026, Kendall’s symptoms became more limiting, prompting a specialist consultation due to increased intensity of pain and new right leg paresthesias. Examination findings documented during that period included a positive straight-leg raise and sensory complaints consistent with an S1 distribution. Detailed imaging was completed to define the anatomic pain generator. A lumbar spine X-ray (03-20-2026) showed mild degenerative changes at L5-S1 without dynamic instability. A lumbar MRI (04-18-2026) demonstrated a right paracentral L5-S1 disc ... (truncated)

images.findings: Five lumbar-type vertebrae. Mild disc space narrowing at L5-S1 with small anterior osteophytes. Alignment is maintained. No spondylolisthesis. Flexion/extension views show no abnormal translational movement to suggest dynamic instability. No acute fracture or destructive osseous lesion identified.

L5-S1: Right paracentral disc protrusion with annular fissure contacting the descending right S1 nerve root in the lateral recess; mild-to-moderate right neural foraminal narrowing. L4-L5: Mild broad-based disc bulge without high-grade stenosis. No significant central canal stenosis. Conus terminates normally. No epidural collection. Findings support targeted image-guided planning for the requested CT guidance for needle placement at the symptomatic level.

265.bio_narrative: Lisa Cuddy is a 57-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas, who works in healthcare administration and is accustomed to managing stress and long workdays. Over the last 7–8 months she developed increasingly frequent heartburn and burning epigastric pain (K21.9, R10.13), initially responsive to over-the-counter antacids but gradually becoming daily and worse at night. She has been consistent with lifestyle measures such as avoiding late meals and elevating the head of her bed. Despite an adequate therapeutic trial of pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily and adjunct sucralfate, she continues to have significant symptoms. Approximately 4 months before the planned procedure date, she noticed progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods (R13.10). She describes a sensation of food sticking in the mid-chest several times per week, requiring water to clear, and she has begun limiting portions to avoid discomfort. This change in intake has been accompanied by unintentional weight loss of roughly 12–14 pounds (R63.4). In the 6–8 weeks prior to gastroenterology consultation, she developed marked fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance and reported intermittent black, tarry stools (K92.1). Laboratory ... (truncated)

275.bio_narrative: Sansa Baratheon is a 70-year-old widowed woman living independently in Houston, Texas. She is soft-spoken, highly self-controlled in clinical visits, and tends to understate symptoms unless specifically asked. Over the last several months she has noticed intermittent upper abdominal discomfort that she describes as “indigestion” along with occasional nausea. The discomfort is typically epigastric (R10.13), comes and goes without a clear pattern, and is sometimes accompanied by a mild burning sensation she calls “heartburn,” consistent with a possible reflux component (K21.9). She has not reported vomiting, and her record does not document alarm symptoms such as hematemesis or melena. She has not tracked weight changes carefully and does not emphasize constitutional symptoms unless prompted. Her first contact for these symptoms was a telehealth visit in December 2025, where she described vague dyspepsia (K30) and occasional nausea (R11.0). She was advised to try dietary adjustments and intermittent OTC calcium carbonate antacid and to follow up if symptoms persisted. At a primary care office visit in February 2026, symptoms were again discussed; the plan focused on basic laboratory ... (truncated)

274.bio_narrative: Ann Perkins is a 69-year-old divorced woman living in Austin, Texas, who is known for being highly organized and proactive about preventive care. She previously worked in public-facing administrative roles and remains active in her community, often coordinating appointments and health tasks for friends and family. Over the past two months, she developed frequent epigastric burning discomfort with reflux symptoms, including sour taste and regurgitation, worse with coffee and late meals. She also noticed fatigue and reduced stamina during her usual walks. She denied hematemesis and did not clearly observe melena, but her primary care physician obtained stool testing due to concern for occult blood loss. On 2026-03-12, her FIT/FOBT returned positive, documented as R19.5 (Other fecal abnormalities). A follow-up laboratory evaluation on 2026-03-14 demonstrated microcytic anemia with low ferritin and low transferrin saturation, consistent with D50.9 (Iron deficiency anemia, unspecified). Her symptoms persisted despite initiation of pantoprazole and focused lifestyle measures. A key contributing risk factor is her long-term NSAID exposure (Z79.1) related to chronic right knee osteoarthrit ... (truncated)

273.bio_narrative: Severus Finch is a 64-year-old divorced man living in Houston, Texas, who presents as reserved and analytical, preferring to keep his health concerns private until symptoms persist. He recently established care with a new primary care physician after relocating within the city to be closer to a small support network. He is meticulous with routines and tends to self-manage minor symptoms with over-the-counter remedies before seeking medical advice. Over the past several months, he has noticed intermittent changes in bowel habits (R19.4) characterized by episodic constipation (K59.00) and variable stool frequency. He describes periods of one to two days without a bowel movement followed by smaller, less satisfying stools, without a clearly identified dietary trigger. Initial primary care evaluation focused on general counseling regarding hydration and fiber intake, with intermittent polyethylene glycol use discussed; no defined course of a structured bowel regimen is documented. Basic laboratory testing (CBC/CMP) was obtained and did not reveal anemia or other specific abnormalities, but targeted GI testing (e.g., inflammatory markers, stool studies) and abdominal imaging are not pre ... 272.bio_narrative: Monica Geller is a 61-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas, who is meticulous about routines and tends to push through symptoms until they disrupt her day. Over the past 5–6 months she noticed a persistent change in bowel habits (R19.4), alternating between constipation with straining and episodes of looser stools. During this period she also began seeing intermittent dark stools and occasional red blood mixed with stool (K92.1). She initially attributed symptoms to stress and diet changes, but over time she developed progressive fatigue and an unintentional weight loss (R63.4), prompting her to seek care. At her primary care visit on 2026-02-18, she described ongoing cramping and bloating with intermittent bleeding. Conservative measures were started, including a bowel regimen with polyethylene glycol and diet counseling, and she used a short course of rectal hydrocortisone for suspected hemorrhoidal irritation (past use). Given the persistence and pattern of bleeding, her PCP ordered a fecal immunochemical test and laboratory evaluation. On 2026-03-06, FIT returned positive and CBC showed a mild microcytic anemia pattern consistent with iron deficiency (D50.9). A CT abdomen/pe ... (truncated)

266.bio_narrative: Roman Wambsgans is a 38-year-old married man living in downtown Austin, Texas, working in a high-pressure corporate operations role that involves long hours, frequent travel within Texas, and irregular meal timing. Over the past several weeks he has noticed intermittent changes in bowel habits (R19.4), describing alternating softer stools and occasional constipation, accompanied by mild, non-localized abdominal discomfort (R10.9). He has not observed blood in the stool and denies melena, persistent diarrhea, fevers, or unintentional weight loss. He reports baseline generalized anxiety (F41.1), and he recognizes that symptom-focused worry tends to escalate during stressful work periods, contributing to repeated checking behaviors and frequent online health searches. He established care with UT Health Austin - Primary Care and was evaluated on 2026-03-04 for these concerns. The abdominal exam was benign, and initial laboratory testing (CBC, CMP, TSH) was ordered; results were within reference ranges. 264.bio_narrative: Carmela S. is a 59-year-old woman living in Dallas, Texas, followed by Dr. Marisol Vega at UT Southwestern Family Medicine at Park Cities. Over the past several months she has described intermittent post-prandial burning in the upper abdomen (R10.13) with a pattern consistent with dyspepsia (K30) and reflux symptoms (K21.9). She notes that symptoms tend to fluctuate with meal timing and the types of foods she eats, especially late-night meals and caffeinated beverages. She also experiences intermittent nausea (R11.0) and uses ondansetron ODT only when symptoms are bothersome. She has not reported gastrointestinal bleeding, persistent vomiting, progressive swallowing difficulty, or other alarm features in her visits documented to date. Her symptom management has largely been episodic. She uses famotidine 20 mg and calcium carbonate antacids as needed, often waiting until discomfort is noticeable before taking medication. She avoids NSAIDs after noticing upper GI upset with ibuprofen in the past and prefers acetaminophen for occasional musculoskeletal pain. 267.bio_narrative: Anthony Stark is a 64-year-old divorced male living in Houston, Texas who works as an engineering consultant and spends long hours at a desk with frequent travel around the metro area. He describes himself as highly driven and detail-oriented, often pushing through symptoms until they become disruptive. Over the last 4–6 months he noted intermittent bright red blood per rectum, typically streaking the stool or on toilet paper, occurring several times per month. During the same period he developed a change in bowel habits with alternating constipation and looser stools and recurrent left lower quadrant cramping discomfort that improves somewhat after bowel movements. He denies fever or acute infectious symptoms but reports increasing concern because of his family history and prior colonoscopy findings. He has a documented personal history of colonic polyps (Z86.010) with a prior colonoscopy in 2022 that included removal of adenomatous polyp(s) and recommendation for surveillance. He also has a confirmed high-risk family history of colorectal cancer in a first-degree relative (Z80.0), which he reports as his father being diagnosed in his early 60s. Given this risk profile and the new ... (truncated)

210.bio_narrative: Oscar Martinez is a 67-year-old Hispanic male living in Dallas, Texas, followed at UT Southwestern Family Medicine by Dr. Karen Fields, MD for chronic cardiometabolic conditions including essential hypertension (I10), type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications (E11.9), hyperlipidemia (E78.5), and obesity (E66.9). He is highly organized with medications and home monitoring, using a pill organizer and recording blood pressure and fasting glucose several times weekly. Over the last several months, his ability to exercise consistently has been limited by chronic right knee osteoarthritis (M17.11), for which he completed a course of physical therapy and uses topical diclofenac and acetaminophen as needed. In early March 2026, he began noticing intermittent tight chest pressure when climbing stairs or walking quickly, often accompanied by shortness of breath (R07.9, R06.02). The symptoms are exertional rather than at rest and have prompted him to slow down, take breaks, and occasionally use sublingual nitroglycerin. He presented to his primary care clinic on 03/12/2026 and later sought emergency evaluation on 03/18/2026 when the exertional pattern persisted. Emergency workup included ... (truncated)

211.bio_narrative: Maxine Porter is a 66-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas who works long shifts in a busy service job and often has to move quickly, carry supplies, and climb stairs. Over the past several months she has noticed episodes of tight, pressure-like chest discomfort with shortness of breath when she is rushing, carrying heavier items, or walking up stairs. The episodes usually improve with rest, but she has become increasingly worried because the symptoms recur unpredictably at work. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol only socially (about 1–2 drinks per month). She reports high situational stress when symptoms occur and is actively engaged in psychotherapy every two weeks, using breathing exercises to help manage anxiety related to recurrent symptoms. She has known essential hypertension (I10) and hyperlipidemia (E78.5), and her weight has gradually increased over the last several years consistent with obesity (E66.9). Her family history is notable for premature ischemic heart disease in first-degree relatives (Z82.49), which has heightened her concern about the current symptoms. On 2026-03-11 she presented to primary care describing exertional chest tightness and dyspnea (R07.89 ... (truncated)

220.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 69-year-old woman in Austin, Texas with end-stage renal disease (N18.6) on a developing renal replacement therapy pathway after progressive chronic kidney disease related to long-standing hypertension and diabetic kidney disease (I12.0, E11.22). Over the last year she has noticed a steady increase in day-to-day fatigue, reduced stamina, and persistent nausea that makes it difficult to keep a consistent meal pattern. She also reports intermittent generalized itching that worsens at night and contributes to poor sleep. These symptoms have been documented across primary care and pre-procedure encounters as part of a dialysis planning process. Her ESRD complications have been managed medically while access planning and scheduling proceed. She takes sevelamer (Renvela) with meals for phosphate and mineral balance support, patiromer (Veltassa) daily to manage hyperkalemia risk (E87.5), and sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis (E87.2). Volume and blood pressure are addressed with furosemide and amlodipine in the setting of hypertensive CKD (I12.0). She uses insulin glargine nightly for type 2 diabetes with diabetic CKD (E11.22), and ferrous sulfate to support anemi ... (truncated)

images.findings: Bilateral kidneys are small with increased cortical echogenicity and cortical thinning, consistent with chronic medical renal disease in the setting of ESRD (N18.6). No hydronephrosis or obstructing stone is identified. Bladder partially distended without focal wall abnormality. Findings support chronicity and exclude obstructive uropathy as a reversible cause in pre-dialysis evaluation.

images.facility: Ascension Seton Medical Center Austin - Vascular Lab
images.findings: Bilateral upper extremity arterial flow is patent with triphasic waveforms at brachial and radial arteries. Cephalic and basilic veins are patent without thrombosis; measured diameters are documented for access planning with compressibility present. No evidence of central venous obstruction on screening views. Study performed to support planning for hemodialysis access in preparation for requested dialysis (CPT 90999) for ESRD (N18.6).

reports.results: Key results summarized: markedly reduced eGFR consistent with ESRD (N18.6); elevated BUN/creatinine; mild hyperkalemia risk (E87.5) with potassium trending high-normal to mildly elevated on repeat check; low serum bicarbonate consistent with metabolic acidosis (E87.2); CBC with normocytic anemia pattern consistent with anemia in CKD (D63.1). Glucose elevated consistent with diabetes with CKD (E11.22).
reports.notes: Labs obtained during ED evaluation for weakness/nausea and used to assess metabolic stability in preparation for initiating dialysis planning. No evidence of leukocytosis or acute infection pattern in CBC.

reports.results: Key results summarized for ESRD planning: persistent severe reduction in eGFR consistent with N18.6; bicarbonate below normal range consistent with ongoing metabolic acidosis (E87.2) despite bicarbonate therapy; phosphorus elevated supporting need for phosphate binder therapy; potassium intermittently elevated supporting ongoing hyperkalemia management (E87.5). CBC shows anemia consistent with D63.1; iron studies used to support ongoing supplementation decisions.
reports.notes: Panel ordered as part of pre-dialysis diagnostic workup and medication management monitoring. Results were reviewed in subsequent visits and incorporated into dialysis start planning and access coordination.

230.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 68-year-old woman living in downtown Austin, Texas, who has historically minimized symptoms until they interfere with daily routines. Over the past 1–2 years she has noticed increasing difficulty chewing and maintaining endurance through a typical meal. She reports that foods requiring sustained grinding (steak, raw vegetables, crusty bread) lead to jaw tightness and fatigue, and she frequently pauses mid-meal or chooses softer textures. This pattern is associated with masticatory muscle soreness that she manages with intermittent analgesics. She describes the problem as functional rather than cosmetic and reports that it affects meal pacing, social dining, and nutrition variety. She also reports chronic nasal obstruction with routine mouth breathing and dry mouth on waking. The nasal symptoms fluctuate but are present most days, and she uses daily intranasal fluticasone and saline spray. Snoring and non-restorative sleep are noted in the record, and she uses melatonin at bedtime when sleep initiation is difficult. She has intermittent throat irritation and a sensation of swallowing effort during periods of reflux symptoms; she takes omeprazole daily which she fe ... (truncated)

She reports ongoing chewing difficulty and jaw fatigue with meals and persistent mouth breathing. She notes sleep initiation issues and reports she stopped taking melatonin because it made her feel groggy in the morning.

231.bio_narrative: Arya Sandoval is a 68-year-old woman living in Fort Worth, Texas who has remained active in her household and community but has gradually limited her diet and social meals due to increasing chewing fatigue and jaw pain. Over the last several years she noticed her bite becoming harder to “find,” with the sensation that her teeth do not meet evenly. During the past 12–18 months, she has developed predictable aching in the jaw and temples by the end of the day, especially after prolonged meals, and she avoids tougher foods because chewing endurance drops and soreness builds. She describes intermittent tenderness over the jaw muscles and TMJ region with a sense of overwork rather than a single acute injury. These symptoms are consistent with jaw pain (R68.84) and temporomandibular joint disorder symptoms (M26.609) in the setting of a malocclusion. She has pursued stepwise conservative care. A night occlusal guard was fabricated and fitted on 2025-11-18, which reduced morning tightness but did not restore comfortable chewing function. For pain flares she uses acetaminophen and topical diclofenac to the jaw/temple area; she previously used ibuprofen for short periods but stopped when it ... (truncated)

Due to anticipated near-term need for dialysis and limited immediate availability of permanent access, a tunneled central venous catheter is requested to enable outpatient dialysis initiation while access planning continues. Comorbid hypertensive CKD (I12.9) and anemia of CKD (D63.1) contribute to reduced physiologic reserve and support timely access planning.

222.bio_narrative: Dina Fox is a 60-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas who has been followed for hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.9) with progression to chronic kidney disease stage 4 (N18.4). She works long, irregular shifts that make routine meal planning and medication timing challenging, and she has developed a strong preference for clear, rule-based instructions to reduce uncertainty when managing health tasks. Over the past several months she has noticed steadily worsening fatigue (R53.83) that limits her stamina during workdays, along with intermittent nausea (R11.0) that tends to be worse in the mornings. She keeps a weekly pill organizer and generally tracks her medications carefully, but she admits missing occasional doses on particularly long workdays. Her care has centered on slowing progression and managing complications of CKD. She remains on amlodipine and furosemide for blood pressure and volume management, and she takes sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis related to CKD. She also takes oral iron for anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1). A prior trial of lisinopril was stopped after concern for worsening renal function and mild hyperkalemia. 2221.bio_narrative: Amelia Shepherd is a 42-year-old woman in Dallas, Texas who has been followed in primary care at UT Southwestern for hypertension that has gradually progressed into hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0). Over the last several years she has noticed a slow decline in energy and increasing difficulty making it through long work shifts. By late 2025, her nephrology workup included a renal ultrasound (completed 2025-09-18), and she began to focus more on diet and blood pressure adherence as her kidney function worsened. In early 2026, she reported persistent fatigue (R53.83) that became daily and began to affect concentration and sleep. She also developed intermittent nausea (R11.0), often worse when she skipped meals or ate higher-sodium convenience foods during busy days. She has been managed medically with antihypertensives (amlodipine and carvedilol) and CKD-directed supportive therapy including sevelamer with meals to support phosphate control and vitamin D supplementation. She takes oral iron (ferrous sulfate) for anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) and uses ondansetron as needed for nausea. She previously used low-dose furosemide intermittently for swelling during CKD ... (truncated)

2231.bio_narrative: Severus Finch is a 66-year-old man living in Dallas, Texas who has been managing progressive hypertensive chronic kidney disease that advanced to end-stage renal disease (I12.0, N18.6). He is dialysis-dependent (Z99.2) and has structured his week around thrice-weekly hemodialysis and medication routines. He keeps written checklists for medications and dialysis-day tasks, and he relies on his sibling for occasional transportation and appointment reminders when fatigue is worse. His current referral is driven by repeated loss of adequate dialysis access performance from his left upper extremity AV graft. The graft was created on 06-18-2025 and later required thrombectomy with revision on 11-14-2025 after thrombosis. Starting in early 2026, he began noticing that during dialysis the staff frequently had to decrease pump speeds due to low flow and alarms, and he was often taken off early. He reported feeling more fatigued and “washed out” after shortened runs, with intermittent swelling between treatments and concern about not receiving the full prescribed dialysis time. A hemodialysis access duplex ultrasound on 04-01-2026 demonstrated low access flow and a hemodynamically significant ... (truncated)

pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient with ESRD (N18.6) has persistent uremic symptoms and complications including recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5), metabolic acidosis (E87.2), and fluid overload (E87.70) with exertional shortness of breath (R06.02) despite medical therapy. Dialysis is being initiated to manage these complications and support safe ongoing care in the setting of hypertensive CKD with ESRD (I12.0) and diabetic CKD (E11.22).
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
E11.22 - Type 2 diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Medical management has included furosemide for volume overload (E87.70), Lokelma for recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5), sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis (E87.2), and anemia management with epoetin alfa and oral iron (D63.1).
pa_request.expected_outcome: Stabilization of potassium and acid-base status, improved volume control and reduction of dyspnea, and improved functional tolerance while continuing management of hypertensive and diabetic CKD comorbidities.

111.bio_narrative: Rosa Diaz is a 67-year-old woman living in Houston, Texas with a long history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes that has progressed to end stage renal disease (N18.6). She has remained very structured in her daily routines, using a pill organizer and home logs for blood pressure and weights, and she tends to minimize symptoms until they clearly interfere with function. Over the last several months, she has noticed worsening fatigue and a growing sense of “brain fog,” along with pruritus that leads to excoriations on her arms. She has also experienced intermittent nausea during periods of worsening uremic symptoms, which previously required a short course of ondansetron that has since been completed. In late March 2026, Rosa presented to the emergency department with significant bilateral leg swelling and shortness of breath when walking short distances (E87.70, R06.02). Since that visit, she has had ongoing volume-related symptoms despite daily high-dose furosemide and strict adherence to a low-sodium diet. Nephrology follow-up documented persistent complications of ESRD, including recurrent hyperkalemia (E87.5) managed with daily sodium zirconium cyclosilicate and metabolic acidosis (E87.2) treated with sodium bicarbonate. Her anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) has required epoetin alfa and iron supplementation, and her blood pressure regimen has needed careful monitoring given the expected hemodynamic shifts with dialysis initiation (I12.0). Her diabetes management has continued with basal insulin and linagliptin (E11.22). Rosa has engaged in weekly renal nutrition counseling, maintaining reduced potassium intake and avoiding high-sodium foods, but her exercise has decreased due to dyspnea and fatigue. She is a former smoker and is firm about avoiding relapse; she drinks alcohol rarely and denies illicit drug use. Family history is notable for diabetes and hypertension in first-degree relatives, which she identifies as contributing to her kidney disease trajectory. Pre-dialysis workup has included laboratory evaluation and imaging consistent with chronic renal disease, and she has completed upper extremity vein mapping with subsequent access planning. Given ESRD with persistent symptom burden and ongoing metabolic and volume complications despite medical therapy, she is being scheduled to begin outpatient dialysis (CPT 90999) with coordinated nephrology, primary care, and vascular access planning.

111.behavioral_notes: Patient is highly disciplined with medications and tends to underreport symptoms until they affect function; she uses a pill organizer and tracks home BP and daily weights most days. Diet adherence is strong with low sodium and reduced potassium intake after renal counseling; exercise has decreased due to fatigue and dyspnea, but she continues short evening walks as tolerated. She does not use illicit drugs and reports rare alcohol use; she is a former smoker and is firm about avoiding relapse. Mood is guarded but stable; she prefers brief, direct communication and benefits from structured plans and clear follow-up steps.

pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient has ESRD (N18.6) due to hypertensive CKD (I12.0) with persistent fatigue, nausea, metabolic acidosis (E87.2), edema/volume overload (R60.0), CKD-related anemia (D63.1), and a prior hyperkalemia episode (E87.5). Dialysis is requested to support ongoing solute and volume management and to guide longitudinal nephrology management as access and supervision logistics are finalized.
supporting_diagnoses: I12.0 - Hypertensive chronic kidney disease with stage 5 chronic kidney disease or end stage renal disease
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Antihypertensives (amlodipine, carvedilol) and diuretic therapy (furosemide) with diet counseling for BP/volume management; oral sodium bicarbonate for E87.2; anemia management with epoetin alfa-epbx and ferrous sulfate for D63.1; past hyperkalemia episode treated in ER with sodium polystyrene sulfonate; lisinopril previously used and discontinued due to advanced CKD and hyperkalemia risk.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Improved control of volume status and uremic symptoms with ongoing monitoring of metabolic parameters and anemia management under nephrologist-supervised dialysis plan.

110.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 57-year-old woman living in Austin, Texas with a long history of hypertension that has progressed to hypertensive chronic kidney disease and now end stage renal disease (I12.0, N18.6). Over the last year, she has noticed increasing fatigue and reduced stamina, initially presenting to clinic on 04-22-2025 with persistent exhaustion and ankle swelling. Despite medication adjustments and attempts at low sodium eating, she continued to report recurrent edema (R60.0) and escalating day-to-day limitations. In early March 2026, her condition became more symptomatic with weakness and nausea, prompting an ER visit on 03-04-2026 where a basic metabolic panel and CBC were obtained as part of renal replacement therapy planning. The ER course documented an episode of hyperkalemia (E87.5) that was treated with a potassium-binding medication, reinforcing the need for close electrolyte monitoring as kidney function declined. Subsequent outpatient labs continued to reflect advanced kidney failure physiology with persistent metabolic acidosis (E87.2), and she began taking sodium bicarbonate as part of ongoing management. She also has anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) with low hemoglobin on CBC, managed with weekly epoetin alfa-epbx injections and oral iron supplementation. A specialist consultation on 03-12-2026 focused on dialysis preparation and access planning, including upper extremity vein mapping ultrasound to support vascular access decisions. She completed a renal replacement therapy counseling/education session on 03-20-2026 to review dialysis logistics and expectations. In follow-up visits through early April, she continued to report fatigue, intermittent nausea, and swelling, and she specifically expressed confusion about who is coordinating and supervising the dialysis plan. A care coordination visit on 04-10-2026 and subsequent coordination documentation emphasized consolidating communication through her nephrology clinic to ensure consistent supervision and a clear pathway to dialysis initiation. Socially, April works in an administrative role with variable hours and reports that scheduling complexity increases stress and occasional missed medication doses when she is away from her pill organizer. She is a former smoker, drinks alcohol only occasionally, and denies illicit drug use. She has been engaged in ongoing psychotherapy every two weeks for stress management, which she describes as helpful during the increase in medical visits. Family history includes hypertension in both parents and a sibling with chronic kidney disease, which has shaped her understanding of the seriousness of ESRD. The current referral for dialysis services reflects persistent symptoms and metabolic/volume complications despite medical therapy and the need for a coordinated, nephrologist-supervised renal replacement therapy plan.

encounters.doctor_note:

She notes intermittent shortness of breath on exertion but denies chest pain, syncope, or fevers. She has a known history of hypertension and has noticed home BP readings trending higher despite adherence.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and leg swelling. Negative for chest pain, palpitations, cough, dysuria, hematuria, and acute flank pain. Reports occasional morning nausea without vomiting.

Physical Exam: BP elevated. Trace to 1+ pitting edema at ankles bilaterally. Lungs clear to auscultation. Heart regular rate and rhythm without murmurs. Abdomen soft, non-tender.

Assessment: Hypertensive CKD with concern for progression toward advanced stage (I12.0). Localized edema (R60.0) likely related to volume status. Plan to monitor renal labs and optimize BP regimen.

Plan: Continue amlodipine and carvedilol; initiate/continue furosemide for edema control. Counsel low sodium diet and daily weights. Arrange lab monitoring and follow-up for CKD management and anemia screening.

images.findings: Bilateral upper extremity vein mapping performed for dialysis access planning. Cephalic vein diameter measured 2.6–3.1 mm in the left forearm with compressibility and no thrombus; basilic vein 3.4–4.0 mm in the left upper arm. Right cephalic vein diameter 2.2–2.8 mm; basilic vein 3.1–3.7 mm. Arterial inflow with triphasic waveforms at brachial and radial arteries. Impression: Vein caliber and patency are suitable for consideration of AV fistula creation, with left arm favored based on vein diameter and course.

pa_request.clinical_justification: Patient with ESRD (N18.6) on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2) has recurrent access mechanical complications (T82.590A) causing frequent alarms and early termination of dialysis sessions with symptomatic volume overload. Comorbid chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) and hypertension (I10) increase risk from under-dialysis, supporting placement of a tunneled dialysis catheter for reliable access while definitive access management proceeds.

D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
pa_request.previous_treatments: Ongoing hemodialysis via left radiocephalic AV fistula created 03-15-2022 with repeated dialysis-unit troubleshooting and evaluation for access dysfunction; urgent hemodialysis coordinated during ED presentation on 04-03-2026; past short course of Lokelma for hyperkalemia prior to dialysis schedule adjustment.
pa_request.expected_outcome: Reliable hemodialysis access to allow completion of prescribed dialysis treatments and improved volume management in the setting of I50.32 and I10.

113.bio_narrative: Corrado Soprano is a 73-year-old man living in downtown Dallas, Texas, who has been managing end stage renal disease (N18.6) requiring chronic in-center hemodialysis (Z99.2). He previously underwent creation of a left radiocephalic arteriovenous fistula on 03-15-2022 and has generally maintained consistent dialysis attendance. Over the last two months, he has experienced progressively frequent dialysis machine alarms and high venous pressures with repeated early termination of treatments, consistent with a mechanical complication of vascular access (T82.590A). He reports leaving the dialysis unit “not fully dialyzed,” with fatigue, headaches, and increasing interdialytic weight gain. In early April 2026, after another shortened dialysis run, he developed worsening shortness of breath and leg swelling and presented to the emergency department on 04-03-2026. Evaluation included a chest X-ray demonstrating pulmonary vascular congestion consistent with volume overload, which was clinically significant given his chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) and long-standing essential hypertension (I10) managed with amlodipine and carvedilol. He received an urgent hemodialysis session coordinated through the ED and noted symptomatic improvement afterward, reinforcing the need for reliable access to complete prescribed ultrafiltration targets. Subsequent workup for access dysfunction included a duplex ultrasound of the left upper extremity hemodialysis access on 04-02-2026 demonstrating flow abnormalities consistent with outflow stenosis, correlating with the unit’s repeated alarms. Pre-procedure labs on 04-27-2026 documented chronic anemia of CKD (D63.1) treated with epoetin alfa during dialysis and mild hyperkalemia tendency (E87.5) previously managed with a short past course of sodium zirconium cyclosilicate and dialysis schedule adjustments. Socially, he is a former smoker who quit years ago and drinks alcohol only on special occasions. He prefers straightforward, practical instructions and does best with written plans. Family history is notable for cardiovascular disease, which he keeps in mind when adhering to antihypertensive therapy. Given recurrent inability to complete dialysis through the existing fistula and the clinical consequences of under-dialysis in the setting of I50.32 and I10, his nephrology and access teams have planned insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) at UT Southwestern Medical Center to provide dependable access while longer-term fistula management is pursued.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: My dialysis access keeps alarming and I can't finish treatments like I used to.
encounters.chief_complaint: My dialysis access keeps alarming and I can't finish treatments like I used to.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 73-year-old male with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) reports increasing frequency of access alarms over the past several weeks with shortened runs. He states staff have to slow the pump and sometimes stop early due to high pressures. He notes mild fatigue after treatments and increased interdialytic weight gain. Denies fevers/chills. Reports occasional cramping when runs are shortened and fluid removal is incomplete.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and mild leg swelling after shortened dialysis. Negative for chest pain, fever, or access site drainage. Respiratory: occasional dyspnea on exertion when above dry weight.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing male. CV: regular rate/rhythm. Lungs: clear, no rales today. Extremities: trace bilateral edema. Vascular access: left radiocephalic AVF with reduced thrill compared to prior per patient; no erythema or warmth.

Assessment: ESRD on dialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) with suspected access mechanical complication (T82.590A) causing repeated alarms and incomplete sessions. Comorbid essential hypertension (I10) and chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) increase risk of symptomatic volume overload with under-dialysis. CKD anemia treated with epoetin alfa (D63.1).

Plan: Order duplex ultrasound of left upper extremity dialysis access to evaluate for stenosis or flow limitation. Reinforce strict dialysis attendance and low sodium diet; review daily weights. Continue current medications including amlodipine, carvedilol, sevelamer, calcitriol, and epoetin per dialysis protocol. Discussed that if access continues to fail, patient may require alternative access including tunneled catheter placement (CPT 36558) to ensure full treatments while access is addressed.
observations: Dialysis unit reports frequent venous pressure alarms and reduced achievable blood flow.
AVF thrill subjectively diminished; no signs of infection.

encounters.follow_up_instructions: Complete duplex ultrasound as scheduled. Call clinic for fevers, access redness/drainage, inability to receive dialysis, worsening shortness of breath, or rapid weight gain. Follow up in 2-3 weeks or sooner if treatments continue to be cut short.

HPI: 73-year-old male with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) presents with dyspnea and worsening bilateral leg swelling after a dialysis session ended early due to access alarms (T82.590A). Symptoms progressed over 24–48 hours with orthopnea and reduced exercise tolerance. Denies fever or productive cough. Reports taking home BP meds.

Review of Systems: Positive for dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, and edema. Negative for chest pain, syncope, fever, and access site drainage.

Physical Exam: Mild respiratory distress with exertion; speaking full sentences at rest. Lungs with mild bibasilar crackles. CV regular rhythm. Extremities with 2+ pitting edema to mid-shins. AVF without erythema.

Assessment: Volume overload in dialysis-dependent patient due to incomplete ultrafiltration from access mechanical complication (T82.590A) with comorbid chronic diastolic heart failure (I50.32) and hypertension (I10). ESRD (N18.6) and anemia of CKD (D63.1) noted.

Plan: Obtain chest X-ray (baseline and evaluation of dyspnea), monitor oxygenation and BP, consult nephrology/dialysis for urgent hemodialysis to achieve ultrafiltration goal. Continue home antihypertensives. Discharge planning to include expedited vascular access follow-up given repeated incomplete dialysis; discuss possible need for tunneled dialysis catheter placement (CPT 36558) to ensure treatment delivery.
observations: Dyspnea and edema temporally associated with shortened dialysis run.
No fever or access site infection signs.

112.bio_narrative: Jesse Pinkman is a 67-year-old man living in Dallas, Texas, followed in primary care at UT Southwestern Internal Medicine Clinic. Over the last year he has noticed worsening fatigue that has gradually limited his ability to complete a full workday and perform routine errands without frequent rest breaks. He describes intermittent shortness of breath with exertion such as walking across a parking lot or carrying groceries, along with periods of muscle cramping that became prominent earlier in 2026. He reports that the decline in stamina has been frustrating and has contributed to irritability and sleep disruption, and he tends to minimize symptoms until they interfere with work. His medical history is notable for hypertensive chronic kidney disease (I12.0) with chronic kidney disease coded as stage 3 (N18.3). In early February 2026 he presented to the ER for weakness and cramping in the setting of hyperkalemia risk (E87.5) and was started on sodium zirconium cyclosilicate. Since then, cramping has been less frequent, but he continues to report significant fatigue (R53.83) and exertional dyspnea (R06.02). He is treated for anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1) with epoetin alfa and oral iron, and he uses phosphate binding therapy and diuretics for metabolic and volume management. He avoids NSAIDs and has used acetaminophen in the past for intermittent aches. Jesse’s diet is inconsistent and often includes processed foods; he reports trying to follow low-potassium guidance but finds meal planning difficult. He is a former smoker and drinks alcohol socially on weekends; he denies current illicit drug use. Family history is notable for hypertension in his father and type 2 diabetes in a maternal uncle, and he reports that several relatives have had “kidney problems” later in life though details are limited. Over the past 6–7 months, the combination of symptom progression and recurrent metabolic concerns prompted nephrology consultation and planning for dialysis access. In March 2026 nephrology reviewed his ongoing symptom burden and current CKD-directed regimen, and access planning was initiated. Pre-procedure labs (CBC/BMP/coagulation on 03-31-2026) and duplex ultrasound mapping of the internal jugular/subclavian veins (04-04-2026) were obtained to support safe placement. He is now scheduled at UT Southwestern Medical Center for insertion of a tunneled central venous catheter for dialysis (CPT 36558) with the goal of establishing reliable access for hemodialysis initiation while continuing management of comorbid hypertension, anemia, and electrolyte risk.

114.bio_narrative: April Ludgate is a 64-year-old woman in Austin, Texas followed by Dr. Priya Nanduri, MD at Austin Kidney Care Associates for end-stage renal disease (N18.6) with dependence on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2). She has been on long-term dialysis using a left upper extremity AV fistula created in February 2022, with subsequent access maintenance including a fistulogram with angioplasty in September 2024. Over time she has reported predictable post-dialysis fatigue and intermittent lightheadedness; in early April 2026 she sought ER care after a marked dizziness episode following dialysis. Her chronic comorbid conditions include essential hypertension (I10), anemia in chronic kidney disease (D63.1), and CKD-mineral bone disorder with secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81). She takes amlodipine, carvedilol, and losartan for blood pressure control with dialysis-day precautions for carvedilol as directed by nephrology. She receives epoetin alfa and IV iron sucrose at the dialysis center per protocol based on hemoglobin and iron indices, and uses sevelamer with meals plus cinacalcet with dinner to address hyperphosphatemia and elevated PTH. She has a history of urinary tract infection (N39.0) treated with short, completed antibiotic courses in the past. In March 2026, she presented to clinic describing persistent exhaustion after dialysis and recurrent urinary symptoms, which prompted reassessment and reinforced the burden of ongoing dialysis dependence. She subsequently attended a transplant surgery consultation on 04-22-2026 to discuss renal transplantation with recipient nephrectomy, and was referred for pre-operative clearance, including cardiology consultation on 05-06-2026 and a formal pre-op visit on 05-20-2026. Pre-transplant diagnostic workup includes CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast (05-08-2026) for anatomic planning and lab evaluation (CBC/CMP/CKD-MBD panel on 05-14-2026) to characterize anemia, electrolytes, and metabolic bone parameters in the perioperative window. Transplant immunology testing (ABO/HLA/PRA/crossmatch on 05-16-2026) was performed as part of compatibility assessment. Socially, she lives in central Austin and relies on a structured routine to maintain dialysis attendance and medication adherence, using a pill organizer. She is typically quiet and sardonic but engages well when care steps are clearly laid out. She reports rare alcohol use and no tobacco or illicit drug use. She has situational anxiety and sleep disruption related to the transplant process and participates in monthly psychiatric medication-management/brief psychotherapy visits, which are being coordinated alongside transplant planning.

therapies.cpt_description: Office or other outpatient visit for the evaluation and management of an established patient, moderate level
therapies.reason: Situational anxiety and sleep disruption related to transplant process; ongoing medication management and brief psychotherapy support
therapies.notes: Patient reports increased pre-procedure worry and fragmented sleep; uses coping strategies and maintains dialysis attendance. No suicidal ideation endorsed; safety plan reviewed each visit.

114.behavioral_notes: Patient is typically quiet and sardonic but engaged in care planning when given clear, concrete steps. She reports taking home medications consistently and uses a pill organizer; dialysis attendance is reliable. Diet is renal-focused with occasional lapses on high-phosphorus snacks; she is receptive to coaching when labs are reviewed. Never smoker, drinks alcohol rarely (1–2 drinks/month), and denies illicit drug use. She reports situational anxiety and sleep disruption related to the transplant process and participates in monthly psychiatric medication-management/brief psychotherapy visits; no suicidal ideation endorsed.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Follow-up for ESRD on hemodialysis with complaints of post-dialysis fatigue and recurrent urinary symptoms.
encounters.chief_complaint: I’m wiped out after dialysis and I keep getting these UTIs.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 64-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2) presents for routine nephrology follow-up. She reports persistent fatigue after dialysis sessions, requiring several hours of rest afterward, and notes intermittent dysuria and urinary urgency similar to prior urinary tract infections (N39.0). She denies fever or flank pain today but reports poor sleep on nights before dialysis.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue, intermittent dysuria/urgency. Negative for chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, fever, chills, and gross hematuria.

Physical Exam: Well-appearing female, no acute distress. Cardiovascular: regular rate/rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no CVA tenderness. Left upper extremity AV fistula with palpable thrill and audible bruit.

Assessment: ESRD on dialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) with dialysis-associated fatigue; hypertension (I10) above goal today; history of recurrent UTI symptoms (N39.0); anemia of CKD (D63.1) on dialysis protocol; secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin (N25.81) on binder/cinacalcet.

Plan: Continue dialysis schedule and review dry weight/ultrafiltration goals with dialysis unit to address post-dialysis fatigue. Continue amlodipine, carvedilol with dialysis-day hold parameters, and losartan; home BP log requested. UA/culture ordered through dialysis unit if symptoms persist; reviewed warning signs (fever, flank pain). Continue sevelamer and cinacalcet; reinforce renal diet. Continue epoetin/IV iron per protocol based on labs.
observations: Reports fatigue after dialysis sessions with prolonged recovery time.
Intermittent dysuria/urgency without systemic symptoms.
AV fistula patent with thrill/bruit.
procedures_performed: 99214 - Office or other outpatient visit for the evaluation and management of an established patient, moderate level
diagnoses: N39.0 - Urinary tract infection, site not specified
D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
N25.81 - Secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin
medications_prescribed: Continue Coreg (Carvedilol 12.5 mg) BID with dialysis-day hold parameter
Continue Renvela (Sevelamer carbonate 800 mg) with meals
encounters.progress_notes: Patient remains adherent with dialysis and medications. Persistent fatigue pattern after dialysis noted; plan focused on dialysis parameter review and symptom monitoring. Blood pressure elevated in clinic; home readings requested to guide medication adjustments.
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Follow up in 4-6 weeks or sooner for worsening symptoms. Seek urgent care/ER for fever, flank pain, severe dizziness, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Evaluation of dizziness and near-syncope shortly after hemodialysis session.
encounters.facility: St. David's Medical Center - Emergency Department
encounters.chief_complaint: I got really dizzy after dialysis and almost passed out.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 64-year-old female with ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) presents after developing sudden dizziness and near-syncope shortly following completion of dialysis. She reports standing up to leave the center and feeling lightheaded with tunnel vision. No complete loss of consciousness. She denies chest pain, new focal weakness, or shortness of breath. She reports similar but less severe episodes in the past after larger fluid removal.

Review of Systems: Positive for dizziness and generalized weakness. Negative for chest pain, palpitations, fever, and focal neurologic deficits.

Physical Exam: Alert and oriented. Mucous membranes mildly dry. Cardiovascular: regular rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs clear. Neuro exam nonfocal. AV fistula site intact without bleeding.

Assessment: Post-dialysis hypotension/volume shifts in patient with ESRD (N18.6, Z99.2). Hypertension history (I10) with current low BP likely related to ultrafiltration. Anemia of CKD (D63.1) may contribute to exertional symptoms.

Plan: Monitored in ED; oral fluids as tolerated and cautious IV fluids given. Orthostatic vitals assessed. Advised to coordinate with dialysis unit and nephrology regarding target dry weight and ultrafiltration goals; review antihypertensive timing on dialysis days. Discharged when symptoms improved and BP stabilized.
observations: Near-syncope following dialysis with low BP on arrival.
No focal neurologic deficits noted.
Symptoms improved with monitoring and fluids.
procedures_performed: 99284 - Emergency department visit for the evaluation and management of a patient, moderate severity
diagnoses: D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
medications_prescribed: No new outpatient prescriptions from ED; advised medication timing review with nephrology
encounters.progress_notes: Symptoms improved during observation with stabilization of blood pressure. Discharge with close nephrology follow-up and dialysis parameter review.
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Follow up with nephrology within 1 week and discuss dialysis ultrafiltration and antihypertensive timing. Return to ER for recurrent syncope, chest pain, shortness of breath, or persistent dizziness.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Transplant surgery consultation for ESRD patient on long-term hemodialysis to discuss renal transplantation with recipient nephrectomy pathway.
encounters.chief_complaint: I’m here to talk about getting a transplant.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 64-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2) presents for initial transplant surgery consultation to discuss renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (requested CPT 50360). She reports significant dialysis-related fatigue and time burden, with intermittent post-dialysis dizziness as recently evaluated in the ED. She has a history of hypertension (I10) and CKD-related anemia (D63.1) treated with epoetin/IV iron at dialysis.

Review of Systems: Positive for fatigue and episodic lightheadedness after dialysis. Negative for current fever or active infection symptoms.

Physical Exam: Well-nourished female, no acute distress. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams unremarkable. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Extremities without edema. AV fistula in left upper extremity with good thrill.

Assessment: ESRD on hemodialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) undergoing transplant evaluation. Comorbid hypertension (I10), anemia in CKD (D63.1), secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81). History of UTI (N39.0) noted with prior treatment courses completed.

Plan: Reviewed transplant evaluation pathway, perioperative expectations, and need for pre-operative clearance including cardiology assessment due to ESRD/HTN risk profile. Ordered CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast for anatomic planning and vascular evaluation. Ordered updated labs including CBC/CMP/CKD-MBD panel and transplant immunology testing (ABO/HLA/PRA/crossmatch). Discussed medication coordination around surgery and dialysis scheduling. Patient voiced understanding and agreed to proceed with workup.
observations: Dialysis dependence with symptom burden reported (fatigue, intermittent dizziness).
No signs of active infection at visit.
Transplant evaluation steps reviewed and testing ordered.
procedures_performed: 99205 - Office or other outpatient visit for the evaluation and management of a new patient, comprehensive
diagnoses: D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
N25.81 - Secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin
N39.0 - Urinary tract infection, site not specified
medications_prescribed: No new medications from transplant surgery consult; continued nephrology regimen
encounters.progress_notes: Patient completed initial transplant consult and agreed to proceed with diagnostic testing and clearance pathway. Coordination initiated with nephrology and cardiology.
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Complete CT imaging and ordered labs; attend cardiology clearance appointment; return to transplant clinic after test completion for review and surgical planning.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Cardiology pre-operative evaluation as part of transplant clearance for requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360).
encounters.chief_complaint: They said I need heart clearance before the transplant.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 64-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) on hemodialysis (Z99.2) referred for pre-operative cardiac assessment prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360). She reports limited exertional tolerance largely due to post-dialysis fatigue and occasional lightheadedness; she denies active chest pain or syncope since the ED visit in April. She has long-standing hypertension (I10) treated by nephrology.

Review of Systems: Negative for chest pain, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Positive for fatigue and intermittent post-dialysis lightheadedness.

Physical Exam: Regular heart rate and rhythm without murmurs. Lungs clear. No peripheral edema. AV fistula present left arm.

Assessment: Pre-operative cardiovascular risk assessment in ESRD/HTN patient (N18.6, Z99.2, I10) undergoing transplant workup. Anemia of CKD (D63.1) may contribute to reduced exercise tolerance.

Plan: Initiated low-dose aspirin (81 mg daily) for cardiovascular risk mitigation with plan for perioperative hold per transplant surgery timing. Recommended continued BP optimization with nephrology. Ordered baseline ECG and transthoracic echocardiogram as part of pre-operative clearance pathway; results to be forwarded to transplant team.
observations: No active angina symptoms reported.
Dialysis-related symptom burden limits exertional capacity.
Cardiac clearance testing ordered in preparation for transplant surgery.
procedures_performed: 99204 - Office or other outpatient visit for the evaluation and management of a new patient, moderate severity
diagnoses: D63.1 - Anemia in chronic kidney disease
medications_prescribed: Ecotrin (Aspirin 81 mg) 1 tablet by mouth daily; hold starting 06-05-2026 for planned 06-10-2026 surgery
encounters.progress_notes: Cardiology consult completed; aspirin started with explicit perioperative hold date tied to planned surgery. Noninvasive testing ordered to support clearance workflow for requested transplant procedure.
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Complete ECG and echocardiogram as scheduled; continue BP regimen as directed by nephrology; notify transplant team of any new chest pain, shortness of breath, or syncope.

encounters.purpose_of_visit: Pre-operative evaluation and medication/dialysis coordination for scheduled renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360).
encounters.facility: St. David's Medical Center - Pre-Op Clinic
encounters.chief_complaint: I’m here for the pre-op visit before the transplant.
encounters.doctor_note:
HPI: 64-year-old female with ESRD (N18.6) on chronic hemodialysis (Z99.2) returns for pre-operative evaluation prior to planned renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) scheduled for 06-10-2026 at St. David's Medical Center. Since last visit, she completed pre-operative labs and transplant immunology testing. She reports stable dialysis attendance with ongoing fatigue after sessions; no current urinary symptoms and no fever.

Review of Systems: Positive for chronic fatigue. Negative for chest pain, dyspnea at rest, and signs of active infection.

Physical Exam: Patient in no acute distress. Heart regular, lungs clear. Abdomen soft and non-tender. No edema. AV fistula left arm intact.

Assessment: ESRD on dialysis (N18.6, Z99.2) preparing for renal transplantation with recipient nephrectomy. Hypertension (I10) and anemia of CKD (D63.1) are being managed; secondary hyperparathyroidism (N25.81) treated with binder/cinacalcet. History of UTI (N39.0) with prior treatments completed.

Plan: Reviewed perioperative plan, dialysis coordination, and medication instructions. Continue antihypertensives per nephrology guidance; dialysis-day carvedilol hold parameters remain in place. Continue phosphate binder and cinacalcet as directed. Aspirin to be held starting 06-05-2026. Confirmed allergy history (penicillin, shellfish, latex) and communicated latex precautions to OR. Reviewed vaccination status. Discussed need for post-operative monitoring and follow-up schedule.

Concurrent Care Reference: Patient is in active Medication Management (Psychiatry) therapy with Dr. Karen L. Patel, MD every 4 weeks for situational anxiety/insomnia related to transplant process; coordination of perioperative communication and post-op support discussed.
observations: Pre-op medication reconciliation completed with specific aspirin hold date.
Latex allergy precautions noted for OR.
Dialysis scheduling coordination discussed for perioperative period.

encounters.progress_notes: Pre-op readiness review completed, including allergy precautions, medication holds tied to surgery date, and dialysis coordination. Patient remains adherent to dialysis schedule and medication regimen; anxiety addressed with concurrent psychiatric care.
encounters.follow_up_instructions: Proceed with final pre-admission instructions from hospital; confirm dialysis timing per transplant coordinator. Report any fever, urinary symptoms, chest pain, or respiratory symptoms immediately. Arrive for surgery check-in per scheduling call.

images.findings: Non-contrast CT of the abdomen and pelvis obtained for pre-operative anatomic assessment prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) in a patient with ESRD (N18.6) on dialysis (Z99.2). Native kidneys are small with cortical thinning consistent with chronic kidney disease; no hydronephrosis. No obstructing ureteral calculus. Aortoiliac vessels demonstrate no aneurysm; iliac vessel caliber appears suitable for surgical planning on non-contrast study. No acute intra-abdominal process identified.

reports.results: CBC: Hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL, Hematocrit 28.6%, WBC 6.8 K/µL, Platelets 212 K/µL (supports D63.1). CMP: Sodium 137 mmol/L, Potassium 5.2 mmol/L, Bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, BUN 62 mg/dL, Creatinine 9.8 mg/dL (consistent with N18.6), Albumin 3.7 g/dL. CKD-MBD: Phosphorus 6.1 mg/dL, Calcium 8.6 mg/dL, Intact PTH 612 pg/mL (supports N25.81).
reports.notes: Collected as part of pre-operative optimization and dialysis management prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360). Anemia management continues with epoetin alfa and IV iron per dialysis protocol; phosphate binder and cinacalcet continued with diet reinforcement.

reports.results: ABO: O positive. HLA typing completed (A, B, C, DR, DQ loci). Calculated PRA: 18%. Virtual crossmatch performed using current antibody profile; no donor-specific antibodies reported on screening panel at time of draw. Serologic screening documented for transplant workup.
reports.notes: Testing performed to support transplant planning and compatibility assessment prior to requested renal transplant with recipient nephrectomy (CPT 50360) for ESRD (N18.6) with dialysis dependence (Z99.2). Results routed to transplant coordinator and surgical team for listing/offer evaluation.

procedures.notes: Post-op course uncomplicated; fistula matured and has been used for outpatient hemodialysis.

procedures.notes: Angioplasty performed with improved flow; continued use for dialysis thereafter.

social_history.family_history_relevant: Father with hypertension; mother with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease later in life; no known familial bleeding disorders.
